metadata.title: HashiCorp Vault Associate VA-002 Practice Questions

8.name: Be Aware of Vault Enterprise Features

questions.question: Which authentication method allows AWS EC2 instances to authenticate to Vault using their instance identity document?
options: AWS auth method
Token auth method
Userpass auth method
TLS certificates auth method
questions.explanation: The AWS auth method allows EC2 instances and IAM principals to authenticate to Vault using AWS-specific identity documents and credentials. EC2 instances use their instance identity document (PKCS7 signature) while IAM principals use STS GetCallerIdentity.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the token auth method in Vault?
options: It is always enabled by default and cannot be disabled
It must be manually enabled after Vault initialization
It is only available in Vault Enterprise
It requires an external identity provider to function
questions.explanation: The token auth method is the only auth method that is always enabled by default and cannot be disabled. All other auth methods must be explicitly enabled. Every authentication request to Vault ultimately results in a token being generated.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an auth method in Vault?
options: To encrypt data at rest within Vault storage
To verify the identity of a user or machine and assign policies
To generate dynamic database credentials
To replicate secrets across data centers
questions.explanation: Auth methods in Vault are responsible for verifying the identity of users or machines (authentication) and assigning a set of policies that define what the authenticated entity is allowed to do (authorization mapping). After successful authentication, Vault issues a token with the assigned policies.

questions.question: Which auth method is most appropriate for human operators who need to log in with a username and password?
options: AppRole
Kubernetes
Userpass
AWS
questions.explanation: The userpass auth method allows human users to authenticate with a username and password combination. AppRole and Kubernetes are designed for machine-to-machine authentication, while AWS is designed for workloads running in AWS environments.

questions.question: Which auth method is specifically designed for applications and automated workflows rather than human users?
options: LDAP
Userpass
AppRole
GitHub
questions.explanation: AppRole is designed specifically for machine-to-machine authentication. It uses a RoleID (like a username) and a SecretID (like a password) to authenticate applications and automated workflows. LDAP, userpass, and GitHub are primarily designed for human authentication.

questions.question: In the AppRole auth method, what are the two credentials required for authentication?
options: Username and password
RoleID and SecretID
Client certificate and key
Access key and secret key
questions.explanation: AppRole requires a RoleID (a static identifier similar to a username, often embedded in the application configuration) and a SecretID (a dynamic credential similar to a password that can be delivered securely to the application). This two-part credential model supports secure machine authentication.

questions.question: How does the Kubernetes auth method validate pod identity?
options: By checking the pods IP address against a whitelist
By verifying the pods Kubernetes service account JWT token with the Kubernetes API
By requiring a username and password from each pod
By reading environment variables set during pod creation
questions.explanation: The Kubernetes auth method validates pod identity by verifying the Kubernetes service account JWT token that is automatically mounted into each pod. Vault communicates with the Kubernetes TokenReview API to validate the token and confirm the pods service account, namespace, and other identity attributes.

questions.question: An organization uses Active Directory for employee authentication. Which Vault auth method integrates directly with their existing AD infrastructure?
options: Userpass
GitHub
LDAP
AppRole
questions.explanation: The LDAP auth method integrates directly with LDAP-compatible directories including Microsoft Active Directory. It allows users to authenticate to Vault using their existing AD credentials, eliminating the need to manage separate Vault-specific passwords.

questions.question: What happens when an auth method successfully authenticates a request?
options: The user gains direct access to all secrets in Vault
Vault generates a token with policies attached based on the auth method configuration
The auth method stores the users credentials in Vault storage
Vault sends the authentication result to an external audit system for approval
questions.explanation: After successful authentication, Vault generates a token and attaches policies based on the auth methods role configuration. The token is returned to the client and used for all subsequent API requests. The policies determine which paths and operations the token holder can access.

questions.question: Which auth method uses client TLS certificates to verify identity?
options: OIDC
JWT
TLS Certificates
RADIUS
questions.explanation: The TLS Certificates auth method uses client-side TLS certificates for authentication. The client presents a certificate signed by a trusted CA, and Vault validates the certificate chain. This method is well-suited for environments where PKI infrastructure is already deployed.

questions.question: What is the difference between the OIDC and JWT auth methods?
options: OIDC supports browser-based redirect flows while JWT only accepts pre-existing tokens directly
JWT supports browser-based redirect flows while OIDC does not
OIDC is only for machine authentication while JWT is for humans
They are identical and interchangeable
questions.explanation: The OIDC auth method supports the full OpenID Connect redirect flow (browser-based login with providers like Okta, Azure AD, or Google), while the JWT auth method accepts pre-existing JWT tokens directly without a browser redirect. Both validate JWT claims, but OIDC handles the interactive login workflow.

questions.question: An organization needs Vault authentication for three use cases: developers using SSO, CI/CD pipelines, and Kubernetes pods. Which combination of auth methods best addresses all three?
options: Userpass for all three use cases
OIDC for developers, AppRole for CI/CD pipelines, Kubernetes for pods
LDAP for developers, LDAP for CI/CD, LDAP for pods
Token auth for all three use cases
questions.explanation: OIDC integrates with existing SSO providers for developer authentication. AppRole provides secure machine authentication for CI/CD pipelines using RoleID and SecretID. The Kubernetes auth method leverages pod service account tokens for native Kubernetes workload authentication. Each method is purpose-built for its use case.

questions.question: Multiple auth methods can be enabled simultaneously in Vault. Where are auth methods mounted by default?
options: Under the /secret/ path prefix
Under the /auth/ path prefix
Under the /sys/ path prefix
Under the /identity/ path prefix
questions.explanation: Auth methods are mounted under the /auth/ path prefix by default (for example, /auth/userpass/ or /auth/github/). Multiple instances of the same auth method type can be enabled at different mount points, such as /auth/ldap-east/ and /auth/ldap-west/ for different LDAP directories.

questions.question: What is the purpose of tuning an auth method mount in Vault?
options: To change which users are allowed to authenticate
To adjust mount-specific settings such as default TTL, max TTL, and token type
To encrypt the auth method configuration data
To migrate the auth method to a different storage backend
questions.explanation: Tuning an auth method mount allows administrators to adjust settings like default lease TTL, maximum lease TTL, audit non-HMAC request keys, listing visibility, and token type (service vs batch). These settings apply to all tokens generated by that auth method mount.

questions.question: A security team wants to enforce multi-factor authentication for Vault access. Which Vault feature supports MFA for login requests?
options: MFA is configured within each individual auth method
Sentinel policies can enforce MFA as a policy check
Login MFA can require additional verification factors after initial auth method authentication succeeds
MFA is only available through external reverse proxy configuration
questions.explanation: Vault supports Login MFA which adds an additional verification step after the primary auth method succeeds. Administrators can configure MFA methods (TOTP, Duo, Okta, PingID) and enforce them on specific auth method paths. This provides defense in depth beyond single-factor authentication.

questions.question: What is an identity entity in Vault, and how does it relate to auth methods?
options: An entity is a type of secret engine for storing identity documents
An entity represents a single person or machine that may authenticate through multiple auth methods, unifying their identity
An entity is an external identity provider that Vault delegates authentication to
An entity is a backup authentication path used when the primary auth method is unavailable
questions.explanation: An identity entity in Vault represents a single user or machine that may have aliases across multiple auth methods. For example, a developer might authenticate via LDAP from the office and OIDC from home. The identity system links these aliases to one entity, enabling unified policy assignment and identity-based access control.

questions.question: Which auth method allows Google Cloud Platform instances to authenticate to Vault using their service account or instance identity?
options: AWS auth method
GCP auth method
OIDC auth method
JWT auth method
questions.explanation: The GCP auth method supports two authentication types: IAM (using GCP service account credentials to sign a JWT) and GCE (using the instance identity metadata from a Compute Engine VM). This enables native authentication for workloads running on Google Cloud Platform.

questions.question: Which auth method allows GitHub users to authenticate to Vault using their personal access tokens?
options: OIDC
LDAP
GitHub
JWT
questions.explanation: The GitHub auth method allows users to authenticate to Vault using their GitHub personal access tokens. Vault verifies the token with GitHub's API and maps the user's GitHub organization and team memberships to Vault policies. This is commonly used in development environments.

questions.question: What is the key difference between human auth methods and machine auth methods in Vault?
options: Human auth methods generate service tokens while machine methods generate batch tokens
Human auth methods typically involve interactive login flows while machine methods use automated credential exchange
Machine auth methods are more secure than human auth methods
Human auth methods cannot have policies attached to them
questions.explanation: Human auth methods (userpass, LDAP, OIDC, GitHub) typically involve interactive flows like entering credentials or browser redirects. Machine auth methods (AppRole, AWS, Kubernetes, GCP) use automated credential exchange where the platform provides identity proof without human interaction. Both are equally secure when properly configured.

questions.question: What format is used to write Vault policies?
options: YAML
XML
JSON or HCL
TOML
questions.explanation: Vault policies are written in HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) or JSON format. HCL is the preferred human-readable format that uses path blocks with capability lists to define permissions. JSON format is also supported and useful for programmatic policy generation.

questions.question: Which built-in policy grants unrestricted access to everything in Vault?
options: default
admin
root
superuser
questions.explanation: The root policy is a built-in policy that grants unrestricted access to all paths and operations in Vault. It is automatically assigned to root tokens. The root policy cannot be modified or deleted. The default policy is also built-in but grants only basic permissions.

questions.question: What capabilities can be assigned to a path in a Vault policy?
options: allow, reject, forward, proxy
get, post, put, patch, delete
create, read, update, delete, list, sudo, deny
select, insert, update, delete, grant
questions.explanation: Vault policies support seven capabilities: create (create new data), read (read data), update (modify existing data), delete (remove data), list (list available paths), sudo (access protected paths), and deny (explicitly deny access, overrides all other capabilities).

questions.question: What does the default policy in Vault provide?
options: Full administrative access to all paths
No access to any paths
Basic capabilities such as token self-management and cubbyhole access
Read-only access to all secret engines
questions.explanation: The default policy is automatically attached to all tokens (unless explicitly excluded) and grants basic capabilities including token self-lookup, token self-renewal, cubbyhole access, and the ability to check capabilities. It provides a minimum baseline of permissions.

questions.question: What does the deny capability do when applied to a path in a Vault policy?
options: It denies access but can be overridden by another policy granting access
It explicitly denies access and takes precedence over all other capabilities on that path
It logs denied access attempts without blocking them
It redirects the request to an administrator for approval
questions.explanation: The deny capability explicitly denies access to a path and takes absolute precedence over any other capabilities, even if another attached policy grants access to the same path. This makes deny the strongest capability in Vault's policy system.

questions.question: In a Vault policy, what does the glob character (*) at the end of a path match?
options: Exactly one character
One or more path segments including slashes
Any string within a single path segment (does not cross slash boundaries)
Only numeric characters
questions.explanation: The glob (*) at the end of a Vault policy path matches any string within a single path segment. It does not cross slash boundaries. For example, 'secret/data/*' matches 'secret/data/myapp' but not 'secret/data/myapp/config'. To match multiple segments, use the + character or define deeper paths.

questions.question: When multiple policies are attached to a token and they specify different capabilities for the same path, how does Vault resolve the conflict?
options: The first policy alphabetically takes precedence
The most restrictive policy wins
Capabilities are combined additively, except deny which always takes precedence
Vault denies access whenever there is any conflict
questions.explanation: Vault uses an additive policy model. When multiple policies apply to the same path, all non-deny capabilities are combined (union). For example, if one policy grants read and another grants read and update, the token has both read and update. However, if any policy specifies deny on a path, it overrides everything.

questions.question: What is the sudo capability used for in Vault policies?
options: To run Vault commands as a different user
To access root-protected API endpoints that require elevated privileges
To bypass all policy checks temporarily
To escalate from a service token to a root token
questions.explanation: The sudo capability grants access to paths that are considered root-protected, such as certain sys/ endpoints for managing auth methods, audit devices, and system configuration. Without sudo, even a policy that grants all other capabilities on a root-protected path will be denied.

questions.question: How are policies assigned to tokens in Vault?
options: Policies are assigned directly to IP addresses
Policies are configured on auth method roles and attached to tokens at authentication time
Policies are inherited from the operating system user running the Vault client
Policies are automatically assigned based on the secret engine being accessed
questions.explanation: Policies are configured on auth method roles (such as an AppRole role or a Kubernetes auth role). When a user or machine authenticates through an auth method, the policies specified in the matching role configuration are attached to the generated token. Policies can also be assigned directly when creating tokens via the CLI.

questions.question: What is path templating in Vault policies?
options: Using variables to generate policy files from templates at deployment time
Using identity-based template parameters in policy paths that are resolved at request time
Creating policy paths that automatically expand to cover all secret engines
Using regular expressions instead of glob patterns in policy paths
questions.explanation: Path templating allows policy paths to include variables that are resolved at request time based on the requesting entity's identity. For example, a path containing the identity entity name template ensures each user can only access their own secrets. This enables writing a single policy that provides per-user access control.

questions.question: An administrator wants to allow a team to manage secrets under a specific KV v2 mount but prevent them from deleting secrets or modifying the mount configuration. Which set of capabilities should the policy grant?
options: create, read, update, list on the secret data path and deny on the metadata delete path and mount config path
root on the entire secret path
sudo on the secret path to allow full management
read and list on the secret path only
questions.explanation: Granting create, read, update, and list on the data path allows secret management, while using deny on the metadata delete path and mount configuration path (sys/mounts) prevents deletion and configuration changes. This follows the principle of least privilege for a team that manages secrets but should not alter infrastructure.

questions.question: What is the effect of a policy that specifies an empty capabilities list for a path?
options: It grants all capabilities to that path
It grants only the read capability by default
It denies all access to that path (equivalent to deny)
It inherits capabilities from the parent path
questions.explanation: An empty capabilities list in a Vault policy effectively denies all access to that path, as no operations are permitted. This is functionally equivalent to specifying deny, though using explicit deny is the recommended practice for clarity.

questions.question: Which statement about the root policy is correct?
options: The root policy can be modified to restrict root token access
The root policy can be deleted if another admin policy exists
The root policy cannot be modified or deleted and always grants full access
The root policy only applies to tokens created during initialization
questions.explanation: The root policy is immutable and indestructible. It cannot be modified or deleted and always grants full access to all paths in Vault. While root tokens have the root policy, it is a best practice to revoke root tokens after initial setup and use purpose-built admin policies instead.

questions.question: How does Vault evaluate policies when no policy explicitly covers the requested path?
options: Access is granted by default with read capability
Access is denied by default (implicit deny)
Access is delegated to an external authorization service
The request is queued for administrator approval
questions.explanation: Vault follows a default-deny model. If no attached policy explicitly grants capabilities on the requested path, the request is denied. This secure-by-default approach means administrators must explicitly grant access to every path a token needs to use.

questions.question: A policy allows list capability on a secret engine path but not read. What can the token holder see?
options: The full contents of all secrets at that path
Only the key names (paths) available, but not the secret values
The secret values but not the key names
Nothing, because list without read is invalid
questions.explanation: The list capability allows enumerating key names (paths) at a location without revealing the actual secret values. This is useful for allowing discovery of what secrets exist while requiring separate read permission to view values. List and read are independent capabilities.

questions.question: Which Vault CLI command uploads a policy written in an HCL file?
options: vault policy create
vault policy write
vault policy upload
vault policy apply
questions.explanation: The 'vault policy write' command uploads a policy to Vault from a file or stdin. The syntax is 'vault policy write <name> <file>'. This command creates a new policy or updates an existing one with the same name.

questions.question: Can the default policy be modified in Vault?
options: No, built-in policies cannot be changed
Yes, the default policy can be modified but not deleted
Yes, but only by root token holders
Yes, but only in Vault Enterprise
questions.explanation: Unlike the root policy, the default policy can be modified by administrators to add or remove capabilities. However, it cannot be deleted. Any changes to the default policy affect all tokens that have it attached, which is every token by default.

questions.question: An operator needs to create a policy that grants access to secrets based on the authenticated users identity group membership. Which Vault feature enables this?
options: ACL policy inheritance from parent tokens
Identity-based policy templating with group metadata
External policy delegation to LDAP
Role-based access control at the storage backend level
questions.explanation: Identity-based policy templating allows paths to include template variables that reference the entity's group membership, metadata, and other identity attributes. This enables dynamic, identity-aware policies where a single policy definition can provide different access based on group membership without creating separate policies per group.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the list capability when applied to the sys/policies/acl path?
options: To allow creating new policies
To allow viewing the names of all configured policies
To allow modifying existing policy content
To allow deleting policies
questions.explanation: The list capability on sys/policies/acl allows the token holder to enumerate the names of all configured ACL policies. This is useful for administrators who need to see which policies exist without necessarily having permission to read or modify their contents.

questions.question: What are the two types of tokens in Vault?
options: Primary and secondary tokens
Service tokens and batch tokens
Read tokens and write tokens
Admin tokens and user tokens
questions.explanation: Vault has two token types: service tokens and batch tokens. Service tokens are persisted to storage, have full functionality (renewal, revocation, child tokens), and support all features. Batch tokens are encrypted blobs that are not persisted, are lightweight, but have limited functionality (no renewal, no child tokens).

questions.question: What is the purpose of a root token in Vault?
options: It provides read-only access to audit logs
It provides unrestricted access to all Vault operations and is used for initial setup
It provides access only to the root secret engine
It provides access to replicate data between clusters
questions.explanation: A root token has the root policy attached and provides unrestricted access to all Vault operations. It is generated during Vault initialization and is used for initial setup tasks. Best practice is to revoke root tokens after initial configuration and generate new ones only when needed using the operator generate-root command.

questions.question: What is the TTL (Time To Live) of a token?
options: The encryption strength of the token
The maximum number of times the token can be used
The duration after which the token expires unless renewed
The network latency threshold for token validation
questions.explanation: A token's TTL is the duration for which it remains valid. After the TTL expires, the token can no longer be used. Service tokens can be renewed before they expire (up to the max TTL), which resets the TTL countdown. Batch tokens cannot be renewed.

questions.question: What happens to child tokens when a parent token is revoked in Vault?
options: Child tokens continue to function independently
Child tokens are automatically revoked as well
Child tokens are transferred to the root token
Child tokens are converted to orphan tokens
questions.explanation: Vault maintains a parent-child relationship between tokens. When a parent token is revoked, all of its child tokens (and their children) are also revoked recursively. This prevents credential leakage when a session ends. Orphan tokens are exempt from this cascading revocation.

questions.question: What is an orphan token, and how does it differ from a regular child token?
options: An orphan token has no policies attached
An orphan token has no parent and is not revoked when any other token is revoked
An orphan token can only be created by the root user
An orphan token automatically renews itself indefinitely
questions.explanation: An orphan token has no parent in the token hierarchy. Unlike regular child tokens, orphan tokens are not revoked when another token is revoked (since they have no parent). They still expire based on their own TTL. Orphan tokens can be created with the sudo capability on the token creation endpoint or through auth methods configured to generate orphan tokens.

questions.question: What is the difference between a tokens TTL and its max TTL?
options: TTL is the initial duration and max TTL is the absolute ceiling that the token can be renewed up to
TTL applies to service tokens and max TTL applies to batch tokens
TTL controls read operations and max TTL controls write operations
They are different names for the same value
questions.explanation: The TTL is the initial validity period of a token. The max TTL is the absolute maximum lifetime of the token. A token can be renewed to reset its TTL, but the total lifetime cannot exceed the max TTL. Once the max TTL is reached, the token expires regardless of renewal attempts.

questions.question: What is a periodic token in Vault?
options: A token that is automatically created on a schedule
A token with no max TTL that can be renewed indefinitely as long as it is renewed within its period
A token that can only be used once per time period
A token that synchronizes its expiration with other tokens
questions.explanation: A periodic token has no max TTL and can be renewed indefinitely, as long as it is renewed within its configured period. Each renewal resets the TTL to the period value. If renewal does not happen within the period, the token expires. Periodic tokens are useful for long-running services that need persistent access.

questions.question: When a service token is created by an auth method, which policies are always included?
options: Only the policies specified in the auth method role
The root policy and the policies specified in the role
The default policy plus any policies specified in the auth method role
No policies are included; they must be added separately
questions.explanation: Unless explicitly configured otherwise, every service token receives the default policy in addition to any policies specified in the auth method role configuration. The default policy provides basic self-service capabilities like token lookup and cubbyhole access.

questions.question: What is a token accessor, and what can it be used for?
options: A token accessor is a backup copy of the token stored in Vaults audit log
A token accessor is a reference to a token that allows limited operations like lookup and revocation without exposing the token itself
A token accessor is the encrypted version of a token used during transit
A token accessor is the username associated with a token
questions.explanation: A token accessor is a secondary identifier for a token that allows limited operations: looking up token properties and revoking the token. The accessor cannot be used to authenticate or perform secret operations. This is useful for administrators who need to manage tokens without having access to the actual token values.

questions.question: A CI/CD pipeline needs a Vault token that works across multiple Vault performance replication clusters and does not need renewal. Which token type should be used?
options: Service token with a long TTL
Periodic service token
Batch token
Root token
questions.explanation: Batch tokens are ideal for this scenario. They are lightweight encrypted blobs that are not persisted to storage, making them available across performance replication clusters without replication lag. They do not support renewal but can be issued with a sufficient TTL. Service tokens are tied to a specific cluster's storage.

questions.question: What is a token role in Vault?
options: A mapping between an auth method and a set of policies
A configuration object that defines parameters for token creation such as allowed policies, TTL settings, and whether tokens are orphan or periodic
A predefined set of CLI commands for token management
A replication role for synchronizing tokens across clusters
questions.explanation: A token role is a configuration object that pre-defines parameters for token creation. It specifies allowed policies, disallowed policies, orphan status, renewable status, token type, TTL, max TTL, and other settings. When creating a token against a role, the token inherits these configured parameters.

questions.question: What is response wrapping, and how does it relate to tokens?
options: Encrypting API responses with the clients public key
Compressing token data to reduce network bandwidth
Wrapping a secret in a single-use temporary token so the intended recipient can unwrap it exactly once
Adding HTTP response headers for token validation
questions.explanation: Response wrapping stores a secret in a temporary cubbyhole and returns a single-use wrapping token. The intended recipient uses the wrapping token to unwrap and retrieve the secret. This provides secure delivery because the wrapping token can only be used once, and the original requester never sees the actual secret.

questions.question: When examining token details with vault token lookup, which field indicates the absolute time after which the token cannot be renewed?
options: ttl
creation_time
expire_time
explicit_max_ttl
questions.explanation: The expire_time field shows the absolute timestamp when the token will expire. If the token is renewable, it can be renewed up until this time is recalculated based on the max TTL. The explicit_max_ttl shows the configured maximum, while expire_time shows the actual calculated expiration time.

questions.question: What is a use-limit token in Vault?
options: A token restricted to a specific IP address range
A token that can only be used a specified number of times before it is automatically revoked
A token limited to reading secrets only
A token that limits the size of secrets that can be accessed
questions.explanation: A use-limit token (created with the num_uses parameter) can only be used a specified number of times. Each API call counts as one use. Once the limit is reached, the token is automatically revoked. This is useful for one-time operations or tightly controlled automation tasks.

questions.question: Which command is used to look up the properties of the currently authenticated token?
options: vault token info
vault token lookup
vault token status
vault token describe
questions.explanation: The 'vault token lookup' command (with no arguments) displays properties of the currently authenticated token including its policies, TTL, creation time, accessor, and other metadata. Adding a token value or using -accessor flag allows looking up other tokens.

questions.question: An application's token has a TTL of 1 hour and a max TTL of 24 hours. The application renews the token every 45 minutes. When will the token ultimately expire?
options: After 1 hour if not renewed
Never, because it is being renewed regularly
After 24 hours from creation, regardless of renewals
After 45 minutes if the application stops renewing
questions.explanation: While the token is renewed every 45 minutes (resetting the 1-hour TTL), the max TTL of 24 hours is an absolute ceiling. After 24 hours from creation, the token expires regardless of renewal attempts. The application must re-authenticate to obtain a new token.

questions.question: How do batch tokens differ from service tokens regarding storage?
options: Batch tokens are stored in an external database while service tokens are in Vault storage
Both types are stored identically in Vault storage
Service tokens are persisted to Vault storage while batch tokens are not persisted and exist only as encrypted blobs
Batch tokens are stored in memory only while service tokens are on disk
questions.explanation: Service tokens are persisted to Vault's storage backend, which means they survive Vault restarts and can be looked up and managed. Batch tokens are self-contained encrypted blobs that are not persisted to storage. This makes batch tokens lighter weight but means they cannot be listed, looked up by accessor, or explicitly revoked.

questions.question: How is a root token generated after the initial Vault setup?
options: By running vault token create -policy=root
By using the vault operator generate-root command with unseal keys
By restarting Vault in root mode
Root tokens cannot be regenerated after initialization
questions.explanation: After initialization, new root tokens can be generated using the 'vault operator generate-root' command, which requires a quorum of unseal key holders to complete. This ensures that no single person can generate a root token alone, maintaining the security of the unseal key distribution.

questions.question: What is a lease in Vault?
options: A contract between Vault and a cloud provider for resource usage
A metadata structure that associates a time duration with a secret, after which Vault may revoke the secret
A payment model for Vault Enterprise licensing
A replication agreement between two Vault clusters
questions.explanation: A lease is metadata that Vault attaches to dynamic secrets and some auth tokens. It includes a lease ID, a duration (TTL), and a renewable flag. When the lease expires, Vault may revoke the associated secret (for example, deleting a dynamically generated database credential).

questions.question: Which type of secret in Vault always has an associated lease?
options: Static KV secrets
Dynamic secrets generated by secret engines like the database engine
Secrets stored in cubbyhole
Encrypted data processed by the transit engine
questions.explanation: Dynamic secrets generated by engines like the database, AWS, and PKI secret engines always have associated leases. When the lease expires, Vault revokes the dynamic credentials (for example, dropping the database user). Static KV secrets and cubbyhole secrets do not have leases.

questions.question: What does renewing a lease do?
options: It creates a new set of credentials to replace the expiring ones
It extends the duration of the lease, keeping the existing credentials valid for longer
It transfers the lease from one token to another
It converts a dynamic secret into a static secret
questions.explanation: Renewing a lease extends its duration without generating new credentials. The existing credentials remain valid for the extended period. Renewal does not guarantee the full requested duration; Vault may grant a shorter extension based on the max TTL configuration.

questions.question: What is the lease ID used for?
options: To encrypt the associated secret
To uniquely identify a lease so it can be renewed, revoked, or inspected
To authenticate to the secret engine that generated the lease
To track billing for Vault Enterprise usage
questions.explanation: The lease ID is a unique identifier that Vault assigns to each lease. It is used with CLI commands and API calls to renew a specific lease, revoke it, or look up its details. Applications must store the lease ID returned with dynamic secrets to manage the credential lifecycle.

questions.question: What is the difference between the default lease TTL and the max lease TTL?
options: Default TTL is for batch tokens and max TTL is for service tokens
Default TTL is the initial lease duration and max TTL is the absolute maximum a lease can be extended through renewals
Default TTL applies in development mode and max TTL in production mode
There is no difference; they are the same value
questions.explanation: The default lease TTL is the initial duration assigned to a lease if no explicit TTL is requested. The max lease TTL is the absolute ceiling for lease duration, even through renewals. A lease can be renewed up to but never beyond the max TTL, at which point the application must request new credentials.

questions.question: Where can lease TTL values be configured in Vault? Select the most complete answer.
options: Only at the system level via the Vault server configuration
At the system level, per mount (secret engine or auth method), and per role within a mount
Only per secret engine mount
Only per individual secret
questions.explanation: Lease TTL values can be configured at three levels: system-wide defaults in the Vault server configuration, per mount when enabling or tuning a secret engine or auth method, and per role within a mount. More specific settings override more general ones, giving administrators granular control.

questions.question: What happens to the associated credentials when a dynamic secret lease is revoked?
options: The credentials continue to work until their next use
Vault instructs the backend system to delete or invalidate the credentials
The credentials are moved to a quarantine area for review
Nothing happens to the credentials; only the Vault lease record is removed
questions.explanation: When a dynamic secret lease is revoked, Vault contacts the backend system (database, cloud provider, etc.) to delete or invalidate the associated credentials. For example, the database secret engine will drop the dynamically created database user, ensuring the credentials cannot be used after revocation.

questions.question: What is prefix-based lease revocation?
options: Revoking leases that start with a specific prefix path, affecting all matching leases at once
Revoking leases in alphabetical order by lease ID
Revoking the first lease in a series of related leases
Revoking leases that were created before a specific date
questions.explanation: Prefix-based lease revocation revokes all leases whose IDs start with a specific path prefix. For example, revoking all leases under the database/ prefix would revoke all dynamically generated database credentials. This is useful for emergency situations or when decommissioning a secret engine.

questions.question: An application retrieves database credentials from Vault with a 1-hour lease. The application is still running after 50 minutes. What should the application do?
options: Do nothing; the credentials will automatically renew
Renew the lease before it expires to extend the credential validity
Request entirely new credentials immediately
Stop all database operations until the lease is regenerated
questions.explanation: Applications should proactively renew leases before expiration. A common pattern is to renew at roughly half the TTL duration. Leases do not auto-renew; the application (or a sidecar agent) must explicitly call the renewal API. Failing to renew means the credentials will be revoked when the lease expires.

questions.question: What is the sys/leases/lookup endpoint used for?
options: To generate new leases for existing secrets
To view details about a specific lease including its TTL, issue time, and renewable status
To transfer lease ownership between tokens
To convert static secrets into leased secrets
questions.explanation: The sys/leases/lookup endpoint retrieves detailed information about a specific lease using its lease ID. The response includes the lease TTL, issue time, expiration time, renewable status, and the associated path. This is useful for monitoring and debugging credential lifecycle issues.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the sys/leases/revoke-force endpoint?
options: To revoke leases even if the backend revocation fails, removing only the Vault lease record
To revoke leases with a higher priority than normal revocation
To force-renew expired leases
To revoke leases across all namespaces simultaneously
questions.explanation: The sys/leases/revoke-force endpoint is used when normal revocation fails because the backend system is unavailable or broken. It removes the lease record from Vault without contacting the backend to revoke credentials. The orphaned credentials in the backend must then be cleaned up manually.

questions.question: If a lease renewal is requested with an increment of 2 hours but the max TTL for the mount is only 1 hour away, what happens?
options: The renewal fails with an error
Vault grants a 2-hour extension, ignoring the max TTL
Vault grants a renewal up to the remaining max TTL (1 hour), not the requested 2 hours
Vault automatically extends the max TTL to accommodate the request
questions.explanation: Vault never extends a lease beyond the max TTL. If the requested increment would exceed the remaining time before max TTL, Vault grants a shorter extension limited to the max TTL boundary. The application receives the actual granted TTL in the response and should handle this accordingly.

questions.question: How does Vault Agent help with lease management for applications?
options: Vault Agent stores all leases in a local SQLite database
Vault Agent acts as a proxy that handles authentication, token renewal, and lease renewal automatically on behalf of the application
Vault Agent replaces the need for leases entirely
Vault Agent converts all dynamic secrets to static secrets
questions.explanation: Vault Agent runs as a sidecar or daemon and handles authentication, token lifecycle management, and template rendering automatically. It can auto-renew tokens and re-authenticate when tokens expire, simplifying application integration so developers do not need to implement lease renewal logic.

questions.question: Can a lease be renewed after it has already expired?
options: Yes, within a 24-hour grace period
Yes, but only with a root token
No, expired leases cannot be renewed; the application must request new credentials
Yes, but the renewal incurs a penalty of half the original TTL
questions.explanation: Once a lease expires, it cannot be renewed. The associated dynamic credentials are revoked (or scheduled for revocation), and the lease record is removed. The application must authenticate again if needed and request new credentials to obtain a new lease.

questions.question: Which CLI command revokes a specific lease by its ID?
options: vault lease delete <lease-id>
vault lease revoke <lease-id>
vault lease expire <lease-id>
vault lease cancel <lease-id>
questions.explanation: The 'vault lease revoke <lease-id>' command revokes a specific lease. This triggers Vault to contact the backend system to invalidate the associated dynamic credentials. Adding the -prefix flag allows revoking all leases matching a path prefix.

questions.question: When a token is revoked, what happens to the leases associated with that token?
options: Leases continue to exist independently of the token
Leases are transferred to the parent token
All leases associated with the revoked token are also revoked
Leases enter a suspended state until a new token claims them
questions.explanation: When a token is revoked, all leases that were created using that token are also revoked. This cascading revocation ensures that dynamic credentials generated during a session are cleaned up when the session ends, preventing orphaned credentials from persisting.

questions.question: What is the relationship between token TTL and lease TTL for dynamic secrets?
options: Token TTL and lease TTL are always identical
Lease TTL is always longer than token TTL
They are independent: a token can expire while its created leases continue, or leases can expire while the token is still valid
Token TTL determines the maximum lease TTL
questions.explanation: Token TTL and lease TTL are configured independently. A token might have a 24-hour TTL while the dynamic secrets it generates have 1-hour leases. However, when a token expires or is revoked, all associated leases are revoked too, regardless of the lease TTL remaining.

questions.question: Which CLI command renews a lease?
options: vault lease extend <lease-id>
vault lease renew <lease-id>
vault lease refresh <lease-id>
vault lease update <lease-id>
questions.explanation: The 'vault lease renew <lease-id>' command renews a specific lease. You can optionally specify an increment to request a specific new TTL. Vault may grant a shorter duration if the max TTL is approaching.

questions.question: What is the KV (Key-Value) secret engine used for?
options: Dynamically generating database credentials
Storing static key-value pairs as secrets
Encrypting and decrypting data without storing it
Issuing X.509 certificates
questions.explanation: The KV secret engine stores arbitrary static secrets as key-value pairs. It is the simplest secret engine and is commonly used for storing configuration values, credentials, and other sensitive data. It comes in two versions: v1 (no versioning) and v2 (with versioning).

questions.question: What is the key difference between KV secret engine version 1 and version 2?
options: Version 2 encrypts data while version 1 does not
Version 2 supports secret versioning and metadata while version 1 does not
Version 1 is faster because it uses a different storage format
Version 2 only works with Vault Enterprise
questions.explanation: KV v2 adds secret versioning, which keeps a configurable number of past secret versions. It also provides metadata (creation time, deletion time, version number) and supports soft delete and undelete operations. KV v1 overwrites the previous value on every write with no version history.

questions.question: What does the Transit secret engine do?
options: Stores secrets in an encrypted database
Provides encryption as a service, encrypting and decrypting data without storing it
Transits secrets securely between two Vault clusters
Generates temporary transport-layer certificates
questions.explanation: The Transit secret engine provides encryption as a service. Applications send plaintext data to Vault, which returns ciphertext (and vice versa for decryption). The encryption keys never leave Vault, and the data is not stored in Vault. This allows applications to encrypt data without managing encryption keys themselves.

questions.question: What is the difference between static secrets and dynamic secrets in Vault?
options: Static secrets expire after a fixed time while dynamic secrets never expire
Static secrets are stored and retrieved as-is, while dynamic secrets are generated on demand with automatic expiration via leases
Static secrets are plaintext and dynamic secrets are encrypted
There is no difference; all Vault secrets are dynamic
questions.explanation: Static secrets (such as those in the KV engine) are stored by users and retrieved as-is with no automatic expiration. Dynamic secrets are generated on demand by engines like the database or AWS engine, come with leases, and are automatically revoked when the lease expires. Dynamic secrets reduce the risk of credential sprawl.

questions.question: How does the database secret engine generate credentials?
options: It retrieves pre-created credentials from a credential store
It creates unique database users with limited privileges on demand and revokes them when the lease expires
It rotates a single shared database password on a schedule
It encrypts existing database credentials for secure storage
questions.explanation: The database secret engine connects to a database using a configured connection and creation statement. When a client requests credentials, it dynamically creates a new database user with the specified privileges. When the lease expires, Vault connects to the database and drops the user, ensuring no long-lived credentials exist.

questions.question: Which secret engine is used to generate short-lived X.509 certificates?
options: Transit
KV v2
PKI
SSH
questions.explanation: The PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) secret engine generates dynamic X.509 certificates. It can act as a root or intermediate Certificate Authority, issuing certificates with configurable TTLs. This eliminates the need for manual certificate management and enables automated certificate rotation.

questions.question: What is the cubbyhole secret engine, and what makes it unique?
options: It stores secrets that are accessible by all tokens in the same namespace
It stores secrets that are scoped to a single token and inaccessible by any other token, including root
It is a temporary storage engine that deletes secrets after one read
It stores secrets in an external key management system
questions.explanation: The cubbyhole secret engine provides token-scoped storage. Each token has its own cubbyhole that no other token can access, not even root tokens. When the token is revoked, the cubbyhole data is destroyed. This makes cubbyhole ideal for temporary, private secret storage and is used internally for response wrapping.

questions.question: Which secret engine allows Vault to generate dynamic AWS credentials?
options: KV v2
AWS secret engine
Transit
Database
questions.explanation: The AWS secret engine generates dynamic AWS access credentials (IAM users, assumed roles, or federation tokens) on demand. Each set of credentials has a lease and is automatically revoked when the lease expires. This eliminates long-lived AWS access keys and enables just-in-time access.

questions.question: What operation does the Transit engine's rewrap function perform?
options: It decrypts data and re-encrypts it with the same key version
It re-encrypts data with the latest key version without exposing the plaintext
It wraps plaintext in a response wrapping token
It transfers encrypted data between two transit engine mounts
questions.explanation: The rewrap function takes ciphertext encrypted with an older key version and re-encrypts it with the latest key version, all without exposing the plaintext. This allows organizations to rotate encryption keys and update stored ciphertext without the data ever leaving Vault in decrypted form.

questions.question: An organization needs to store credit card numbers in a way that preserves the format (16 digits) but renders them useless if the database is breached. Which Vault feature addresses this?
options: KV v2 with access control policies
Transit engine standard encryption
Format Preserving Encryption (FPE) via the Transform engine
Tokenization via the database engine
questions.explanation: Format Preserving Encryption (FPE), available through the Transform secret engine (Enterprise), encrypts data while preserving its original format. A 16-digit credit card number is encrypted into a different 16-digit number, maintaining database schema compatibility while rendering the data useless without the decryption key.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a database secret engine connection rotation?
options: To switch which database server Vault connects to
To rotate the root credentials that Vault uses to connect to the database, ensuring even Vault administrators cannot use the old credentials directly
To migrate the database engine to a new mount point
To change the lease TTL for all existing credentials
questions.explanation: Connection rotation changes the root credentials that Vault uses to manage the database connection. After rotation, only Vault knows the new credentials. This prevents database administrators from using the Vault connection credentials directly and ensures Vault is the sole path for credential management.

questions.question: Which command enables a new secret engine at a specified path?
options: vault secrets create -path=my-secrets kv-v2
vault secrets enable -path=my-secrets kv-v2
vault mount -type=kv-v2 my-secrets
vault engine start -path=my-secrets kv-v2
questions.explanation: The 'vault secrets enable' command enables a new secret engine at a specified mount path. The -path flag specifies the mount point (defaults to the engine name). Multiple instances of the same engine type can be enabled at different paths for organizational purposes.

questions.question: What is the SSH secret engine used for?
options: Storing SSH private keys in the KV store
Dynamically generating signed SSH certificates or one-time passwords for SSH access
Encrypting SSH traffic between Vault nodes
Tunneling Vault API traffic over SSH
questions.explanation: The SSH secret engine provides two modes: signed certificates (Vault signs SSH public keys with a trusted CA, and SSH servers trust the CA) and one-time passwords (Vault generates a password that can be used exactly once to log in). The signed certificates mode is preferred as it does not require a Vault helper on the target host.

questions.question: What is the TOTP secret engine used for?
options: Generating time-based one-time passwords for two-factor authentication
Encrypting data with time-locked keys
Creating temporary tokens that expire after one use
Synchronizing Vault cluster clocks
questions.explanation: The TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) secret engine can both generate and validate TOTP codes. It can act as either a TOTP code generator (like Google Authenticator) or a TOTP code validator for services that need to verify TOTP tokens. This centralizes MFA seed management within Vault.

questions.question: Can multiple instances of the same secret engine type be enabled simultaneously?
options: No, each engine type can only be enabled once
Yes, each instance is mounted at a different path and configured independently
Only in Vault Enterprise with namespaces
Only if they connect to different backend systems
questions.explanation: Multiple instances of the same secret engine type can be enabled at different mount paths. For example, you could have database engines at database-prod/ and database-staging/ with different connection configurations and access policies. Each mount is independent.

questions.question: What is the LDAP secret engine (not auth method) used for?
options: Authenticating users against an LDAP directory
Dynamically managing LDAP passwords and generating dynamic LDAP credentials
Storing LDAP directory data in Vault
Replicating Vault data to an LDAP server
questions.explanation: The LDAP secret engine (distinct from the LDAP auth method) manages LDAP credentials. It can rotate existing LDAP account passwords (static roles) or dynamically create and delete LDAP entries (dynamic roles). This brings the same dynamic credential benefits to LDAP/AD environments.

questions.question: What happens when a secret engine is disabled in Vault?
options: The secrets are preserved but hidden from users
All secrets, configuration, and leases associated with that mount are revoked and removed
The engine is paused and can be re-enabled later with data intact
The engine continues to serve existing leases but stops creating new ones
questions.explanation: Disabling a secret engine is a destructive operation. All secrets, configuration data, and active leases associated with that engine mount are revoked and permanently removed. This operation cannot be undone. If the data is needed, it should be migrated before the engine is disabled.

questions.question: An organization wants to use Vault to sign and verify JWTs for their microservices. Which secret engine supports this?
options: KV v2 for storing JWT signing keys
Transit engine for signing operations
Identity secret engine for JWT/OIDC tokens
PKI engine for JWT certificate-based signing
questions.explanation: The Identity secret engine (specifically the OIDC provider feature) can issue signed JWTs. The Transit engine can also sign arbitrary data (including JWT payloads) using its sign/verify operations. For standards-compliant OIDC tokens, the Identity engine is the purpose-built solution.

questions.question: What is the purpose of configuring a max_versions setting on a KV v2 secret engine?
options: To limit the number of different secrets that can be stored
To control how many historical versions of each secret are retained before the oldest is permanently deleted
To set the maximum file size for secrets
To limit the number of times a secret can be read
questions.explanation: The max_versions setting on KV v2 determines how many versions of each secret are retained. When a new version is written and the count exceeds max_versions, the oldest version is permanently deleted. This helps manage storage usage while maintaining a useful history for auditing and rollback purposes.

questions.question: What does the delete operation do to a KV v2 secret versus the destroy operation?
options: They are identical operations
Delete performs a soft delete (version can be undeleted) while destroy permanently removes the version data
Delete removes the secret and destroy removes the entire engine
Delete works on single versions and destroy works on all versions
questions.explanation: In KV v2, delete performs a soft delete that marks the version as deleted but retains the data, allowing it to be recovered with an undelete operation. Destroy permanently removes the version data, making it unrecoverable. This two-tier deletion model supports both operational recovery and compliance requirements.

questions.question: Which environment variable specifies the Vault server address for CLI commands?
options: VAULT_TOKEN
VAULT_ADDR
VAULT_HOST
VAULT_SERVER
questions.explanation: The VAULT_ADDR environment variable specifies the URL of the Vault server that CLI commands connect to. It should be set to the full URL including protocol and port, for example https://vault.example.com:8200. Without this, the CLI defaults to https://127.0.0.1:8200.

questions.question: Which environment variable is used to authenticate CLI commands with a Vault token?
options: VAULT_ADDR
VAULT_AUTH
VAULT_TOKEN
VAULT_SECRET
questions.explanation: The VAULT_TOKEN environment variable provides the authentication token used by CLI commands. Alternatively, after running 'vault login', the token is stored in a token helper (typically a file) and used automatically for subsequent commands.

questions.question: Which CLI command initializes a new Vault server?
options: vault start
vault operator init
vault server -init
vault setup
questions.explanation: The 'vault operator init' command initializes a new Vault server, generating the initial root token and unseal keys. Initialization happens once per Vault cluster and sets up the encryption key, master key, and key shares according to the specified parameters.

questions.question: What does the 'vault status' command display?
options: The list of all secrets stored in Vault
Information about the Vault server including seal status, version, cluster information, and HA mode
The current user's token details and policies
A list of all active leases
questions.explanation: The 'vault status' command displays server information including whether Vault is initialized, sealed or unsealed, the total and threshold number of unseal key shares, the Vault version, cluster name, and HA (high availability) mode status. This is typically the first command run to check server health.

questions.question: Which CLI command is used to unseal Vault?
options: vault unlock
vault operator unseal
vault open
vault start
questions.explanation: The 'vault operator unseal' command provides an unseal key share to begin or continue the unseal process. Multiple key holders must each provide their key share until the threshold is reached, at which point Vault unseals and becomes operational.

questions.question: Which CLI command reads a secret from the KV v2 secret engine?
options: vault read secret/myapp/config
vault kv get secret/myapp/config
vault secret read myapp/config
vault get kv/secret/myapp/config
questions.explanation: The 'vault kv get' command reads secrets from a KV secret engine. For KV v2, the path automatically includes the data/ segment. The 'vault read' command also works but requires including the full API path (secret/data/myapp/config for v2).

questions.question: Which CLI command writes a key-value pair to the KV v2 secret engine?
options: vault kv put secret/myapp password=example123
vault write kv/myapp password=example123
vault kv set secret/myapp password=example123
vault store secret/myapp password=example123
questions.explanation: The 'vault kv put' command writes secrets to a KV secret engine. Key-value pairs are specified as key=value arguments. For KV v2, this creates a new version of the secret. Multiple key-value pairs can be written in a single command.

questions.question: How do you authenticate to Vault via the CLI using the userpass auth method?
options: vault auth -method=userpass username=admin
vault login -method=userpass username=admin
vault connect -auth=userpass user=admin
vault login userpass/admin
questions.explanation: The 'vault login -method=userpass username=admin' command authenticates using the userpass auth method. Vault will prompt for the password interactively. The -method flag specifies which auth method to use, and additional parameters vary by auth method.

questions.question: Which CLI command lists all enabled secret engines?
options: vault secrets list
vault mounts show
vault engines list
vault list sys/mounts
questions.explanation: The 'vault secrets list' command displays all enabled secret engines including their mount path, type, description, and configuration. This helps administrators understand the current Vault configuration and which secret engines are available.

questions.question: Which CLI command lists all enabled auth methods?
options: vault auth methods
vault auth list
vault list auth/
vault auth show
questions.explanation: The 'vault auth list' command displays all enabled authentication methods including their mount path, type, and description. This is useful for understanding which authentication methods are available and how they are configured.

questions.question: What does the -format=json flag do when added to a Vault CLI command?
options: It sends the request body as JSON
It outputs the command response in JSON format instead of the default table format
It reads input from a JSON file
It validates that the server response is valid JSON
questions.explanation: The -format=json flag changes the CLI output format to JSON, which is useful for scripting and automation. Other supported formats include table (default), yaml, and pretty. JSON output is easier to parse programmatically with tools like jq.

questions.question: Which CLI command is used to seal a running Vault server?
options: vault operator lock
vault operator seal
vault server stop
vault operator shutdown
questions.explanation: The 'vault operator seal' command seals a running Vault server. Once sealed, Vault cannot process requests until it is unsealed again. This is useful in emergency situations where you need to lock down Vault immediately. Sealing requires a token with sudo access to the sys/seal endpoint.

questions.question: Which CLI command creates a new token?
options: vault token generate
vault token new
vault token create
vault token issue
questions.explanation: The 'vault token create' command creates a new token. Options include specifying policies (-policy), TTL (-ttl), whether it is an orphan (-orphan), use limit (-use-limit), and many other parameters. The new token is a child of the creating token unless created as an orphan.

questions.question: How can you extract a specific field value from a Vault CLI command output?
options: Using the -field flag to extract a single field value
Using the -key flag to filter output
Using the -select flag to choose fields
Field extraction requires a separate jq command only
questions.explanation: The -field flag extracts a single field value from the command output, returning just the raw value without formatting. For example, 'vault kv get -field=password secret/myapp' returns only the password value. This is useful in shell scripts where you need to capture a specific value.

questions.question: Which environment variable disables TLS certificate verification for Vault CLI connections?
options: VAULT_TLS_SKIP
VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY
VAULT_INSECURE
VAULT_NO_TLS
questions.explanation: The VAULT_SKIP_VERIFY environment variable disables TLS certificate verification. This is sometimes used in development environments with self-signed certificates. It should never be used in production as it makes the connection vulnerable to interception attacks.

questions.question: What does 'vault operator generate-root' do?
options: Generates a new root certificate for TLS
Creates a new root CA in the PKI engine
Initiates the process to generate a new root token using unseal keys
Generates a root password for the storage backend
questions.explanation: The 'vault operator generate-root' command initiates or continues the process of generating a new root token. It requires a quorum of unseal key holders to provide their shares (similar to unsealing). This is the proper way to generate root tokens after initialization rather than storing the initial root token.

questions.question: Which CLI command deletes a specific version of a KV v2 secret?
options: vault kv delete -versions=3 secret/myapp
vault kv remove -v=3 secret/myapp
vault delete secret/data/myapp/v3
vault kv purge -version=3 secret/myapp
questions.explanation: The 'vault kv delete -versions=3 secret/myapp' command soft-deletes version 3 of the secret. The data can be recovered with 'vault kv undelete'. To permanently destroy version data, use 'vault kv destroy -versions=3 secret/myapp' instead.

questions.question: Which CLI command displays help information for Vault commands?
options: vault --help or vault <command> -h
vault man <command>
vault info <command>
vault docs <command>
questions.explanation: Running 'vault --help' shows top-level help, and adding -h or --help to any command (for example 'vault kv put -h') shows detailed help for that specific command including usage, flags, and examples.

questions.question: Which environment variable specifies a custom CA certificate for Vault CLI TLS connections?
options: VAULT_TLS_CERT
VAULT_CACERT
VAULT_SSL_CA
VAULT_ROOT_CERT
questions.explanation: The VAULT_CACERT environment variable specifies the path to a PEM-encoded CA certificate file used to verify the Vault server's TLS certificate. This is essential in environments using private CAs where the system trust store does not include the Vault server's CA.

questions.question: What is the first step when accessing the Vault UI in a browser?
options: Enter a secret path to browse
Select an authentication method and provide credentials to log in
Configure the Vault server address
Upload a policy file
questions.explanation: The Vault UI presents a login screen as the first step. Users must select an authentication method (token, userpass, LDAP, OIDC, etc.) from a dropdown and provide the appropriate credentials. After successful authentication, the UI displays available resources based on the token's policies.

questions.question: Which Vault UI section allows browsing and managing key-value secrets?
options: The Policies section
The Secrets Engines section
The Access section
The Tools section
questions.explanation: The Secrets Engines section of the Vault UI lists all enabled secret engine mounts. Clicking on a KV engine mount allows browsing, creating, editing, and deleting secrets within that engine. The UI provides a folder-like navigation experience for organizing secrets.

questions.question: Can you view previous versions of a KV v2 secret through the Vault UI?
options: No, version history is only available via the CLI
Yes, the UI shows a version history dropdown with timestamps for each version
Only if you have the root policy
Only for the last two versions
questions.explanation: The Vault UI provides a version history dropdown for KV v2 secrets. Users can view any stored version, see the creation timestamp and metadata, and compare values across versions. This makes it easy to audit changes and roll back to previous values.

questions.question: How does the Vault UI indicate whether the server is sealed or unsealed?
options: A red or green status bar at the top of the page
The login page shows the seal status, and a sealed server presents an unseal interface instead of a login form
The URL changes from /sealed to /unsealed
There is no visual indication; you must check the CLI
questions.explanation: When Vault is sealed, the UI displays an unseal interface that prompts for unseal key shares instead of the normal login form. After sufficient unseal keys are provided and Vault is unsealed, the interface switches to the standard authentication login page.

questions.question: Which Vault UI section is used to manage authentication methods?
options: Secrets Engines
Access
Policies
Tools
questions.explanation: The Access section of the Vault UI manages authentication methods, entities, groups, and leases. From here, administrators can enable new auth methods, configure existing ones, manage identity entities and groups, and view active leases.

questions.question: Can Vault policies be created and edited through the UI?
options: No, policies can only be managed via the CLI or API
Yes, the Policies section provides a built-in editor for creating and modifying HCL policies
Only viewing is supported; editing requires the CLI
Only JSON policies can be edited in the UI, not HCL
questions.explanation: The Vault UI includes a policy editor in the Policies section that supports creating and editing HCL policies. The editor includes syntax highlighting and basic validation. Administrators can create new policies, view existing ones, and modify them directly in the browser.

questions.question: What functionality does the Vault UI 'Tools' section provide?
options: Server configuration and tuning options
Utility functions like wrapping, looking up tokens, generating random bytes, and hashing data
A terminal for running CLI commands in the browser
Network diagnostic tools for Vault cluster connectivity
questions.explanation: The Vault UI Tools section provides utility functions including response wrapping (wrap/unwrap), token lookup, random byte generation, and data hashing. These are commonly needed operations that supplement the main secrets and access management workflows.

questions.question: How does the Vault UI handle wrapping tokens?
options: Wrapping is not available through the UI
The Tools section allows wrapping data in a single-use token and also unwrapping existing wrapping tokens
Wrapping is automatic for all secrets viewed in the UI
Only unwrapping is available; wrapping requires the CLI
questions.explanation: The Vault UI Tools section supports both wrapping and unwrapping operations. Users can enter data to be wrapped (receiving a single-use wrapping token) or paste a wrapping token to unwrap and reveal the contained secret. This enables secure secret sharing directly through the browser interface.

questions.question: What is displayed when a user navigates to a path they do not have permission to access in the Vault UI?
options: The secret values are shown but grayed out
The UI crashes with a server error
A permission denied message indicating the token lacks the required capabilities
The path appears empty as if no secrets exist
questions.explanation: The Vault UI displays a permission denied error when users attempt to access paths their token does not have capabilities for. The UI respects the same policy enforcement as the CLI and API. Users only see and can interact with resources their policies allow.

questions.question: How can an administrator enable a new secret engine through the Vault UI?
options: Secret engines cannot be enabled through the UI
By navigating to Secrets Engines and clicking 'Enable new engine', then selecting the engine type and configuring it
By editing the Vault server configuration file through the UI
By pasting a CLI command into the UI terminal
questions.explanation: The Vault UI provides a guided workflow for enabling new secret engines. Administrators navigate to the Secrets Engines section, click 'Enable new engine', choose from the available engine types, specify a mount path, and configure engine-specific options before enabling it.

questions.question: Does the Vault UI support configuring database secret engine connections?
options: No, database connections must be configured via CLI or API only
Yes, the UI provides forms for configuring database connections, roles, and credential generation
Only viewing existing connections is supported
Only PostgreSQL connections can be configured through the UI
questions.explanation: The Vault UI supports full configuration of database secret engine connections. Administrators can add database connections (specifying plugin, connection URL, and allowed roles), create and configure roles (with creation and revocation statements), and generate credentials through the UI forms.

questions.question: How does the Vault UI display token information for the currently logged-in user?
options: Token information is not accessible through the UI
A token icon or user menu in the upper right shows token details including policies, TTL, accessor, and other metadata
Token details are only shown during initial login
Token information is displayed in the browser address bar
questions.explanation: The Vault UI displays a user/token menu (typically in the upper right corner) that shows the current token's details including attached policies, token accessor, TTL remaining, creation time, and whether the token is renewable. This helps users understand their current access level.

questions.question: Can the Vault UI be disabled if an organization only wants CLI and API access?
options: No, the UI is always enabled and cannot be turned off
Yes, the UI can be disabled in the Vault server configuration by setting ui = false
The UI is a separate application that must be uninstalled
The UI can only be restricted via network firewall rules
questions.explanation: The Vault UI can be disabled by setting 'ui = false' in the Vault server configuration file. When disabled, the Vault server does not serve UI assets, and accessing the web interface returns an error. Organizations may disable it in production for security hardening.

questions.question: What happens in the Vault UI when the current token is about to expire?
options: Nothing, the session silently ends
The UI may display a notification about the expiring token and prompt the user to renew or re-authenticate
The UI automatically renews the token without user interaction
The browser tab is automatically closed
questions.explanation: The Vault UI monitors the current token's TTL and notifies the user when it is about to expire. Users can choose to renew the token (if it is renewable) or re-authenticate. If the token expires without renewal, the UI redirects to the login page.

questions.question: Which authentication method is typically the simplest to use when first logging into the Vault UI?
options: AppRole
Kubernetes
Token
AWS
questions.explanation: Token authentication is the simplest UI login method because it requires only pasting a valid Vault token. This is commonly used during initial setup with the root token. For regular use, methods like OIDC (which provides a browser-based SSO flow) are more user-friendly.

questions.question: How does OIDC authentication work in the Vault UI?
options: Users paste an OIDC token from their identity provider
The UI redirects to the configured identity provider for login, then completes authentication after the redirect back
Users enter their identity provider username and password directly in the Vault UI
OIDC authentication is not supported in the UI
questions.explanation: When OIDC is selected as the auth method in the Vault UI, clicking 'Sign in' opens a popup or redirect to the configured identity provider (Okta, Azure AD, etc.). After the user authenticates with the provider, the browser is redirected back to Vault with an authorization code that Vault exchanges for identity information and issues a token.

questions.question: Can identity entities and groups be managed through the Vault UI?
options: No, identity management is API-only
Yes, the Access section includes interfaces for creating and managing entities, aliases, and groups
Only entity viewing is supported, not creation or editing
Only in Vault Enterprise with the governance module
questions.explanation: The Vault UI Access section provides full identity management capabilities. Administrators can create and manage entities (with metadata and aliases), create and manage groups (both internal and external), and view the relationship between entities, aliases, and auth method mounts.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Replication' section in the Vault UI?
options: To replicate UI configuration across browser sessions
To view and manage Vault cluster replication status for disaster recovery and performance replication (Enterprise)
To synchronize policies between different Vault namespaces
To back up secret data to an external storage system
questions.explanation: The Replication section in the Vault UI (available in Enterprise) displays the status of disaster recovery (DR) and performance replication clusters. Administrators can view replication lag, promote secondaries, and manage replication connections. This is critical for monitoring multi-datacenter Vault deployments.

questions.question: What are namespaces in Vault Enterprise?
options: DNS names used to access different Vault clusters
Isolated environments within a single Vault cluster that provide multi-tenancy with separate policies, auth methods, and secret engines
Labels applied to secrets for organizational purposes
Different Vault server processes running on the same machine
questions.explanation: Namespaces provide tenant isolation within a single Vault cluster. Each namespace has its own policies, auth methods, secret engines, and identity entities, completely isolated from other namespaces. This allows multiple teams or organizations to share Vault infrastructure without interfering with each other.

questions.question: Which Vault deployment option is managed by HashiCorp and does not require the organization to operate Vault infrastructure?
options: Vault OSS (Open Source)
Vault Enterprise self-managed
HCP Vault (HashiCorp Cloud Platform)
Vault Dev Server
questions.explanation: HCP Vault (HashiCorp Cloud Platform) is a fully managed service where HashiCorp operates the Vault infrastructure. Organizations do not need to manage servers, storage backends, upgrades, or high availability. HCP Vault provides Enterprise features in a cloud-managed model.
